Name: Acrion
Notes: visited by Plato

Name: Adrastus of Aphrodisias
Notes: wrote commentaries on Aristotle's works and a commentary on Plato's Timaeus

Name: Aedesius
Notes: studied under Iamblichus before founding his own school in Pergamum

Name: Aeneas of Gaza
Notes: Christian convert who studied under Hierocles

Name: Aenesidemus
Notes:
wrote a book called *Pyrrhonist Discourses* which became a central text for 
the Pyrrhonists

Name: Aesara
Notes: wrote On Human Nature, of which a fragment is preserved by Stobaeus

Name: Aeschines of Neapolis
Notes:
shared the leadership of the Academy at Athens together with Charmadas and 
Clitomachus about 110 BC

Name: Aeschines of Sphettus
Notes: part of Socrates' circle and likely present at his death

Name: Aetius
Notes: Antiochean convert to Christianity who studied in Alexandria

Name: Agapius
Notes: studied under Marinus of Neapolis. known for his learning

Name: Agathobulus
Notes: known for his severe asceticism and teacher of Demonax

Name: Agathosthenes
Notes:
referred to by Tzetzes as his authority in matters connected with 
geography.

Name: Agrippa the Skeptic
Notes: thought to be the creator of the "five grounds of doubt"

Name: Al-Farabi
Notes: First major Islamic philosopher; harmonised Plato and Aristotle; known as the 'Second Teacher' (after Aristotle); influenced Avicenna and Islamic political philosophy

Name: Al-Kindi
Notes: Arab polymath; known as the 'First Arab Philosopher'; synthesised Greek philosophy (especially Neoplatonism and Aristotelianism) with Islamic theology; wrote On First Philosophy; first systematic translator and transmitter of Greek philosophy into Arabic

Name: Albertus Magnus
Notes: Dominican friar and polymath; teacher of Thomas Aquinas; first to systematically introduce Aristotelian philosophy into Latin Christendom; wrote commentaries on all of Aristotle; patron saint of natural scientists; also interested in Neoplatonism and Pseudo-Dionysius

Name: Alcibiades
Notes: Athenian General and Politician

Name: Alcmaeon of Croton
Notes: interested in medicine

Name: Aleksei Losev
Notes: Russian philosopher; last great classical Russian Platonist; wrote an 8-volume History of Ancient Aesthetics; persecuted during Stalin era; specialist in Plato and Neoplatonism

Name: Alexamenus of Teos
Notes: may have been the first to write philosophical dialogues

Name: Alexander of Aegae
Notes: tutored the emperor Nero

Name: Alexander of Aphrodisias
Notes: influential commentator on the Corpus Aristotelicum

Name: Alexander Peloplaton
Notes: Minor Middle Platonist; nicknamed 'Peloplaton' (Clay-Plato)

Name: Alexinus
Notes: founded his own school which did not fare well

Name: Amelius
Notes: student of Plotinus who wrote voluminously

Name: Ammonius of Athens
Notes: teacher of Plutarch

Name: Ammonius Saccas
Notes: Plotinus' teacher

Name: Anaxagoras
Notes: Proposed that the sun is a fiery stone; introduced nous (mind) as the ordering principle of the cosmos; teacher of Pericles and Euripides

Name: Anaxarchus
Notes: Companion of Alexander the Great; known for equanimity in the face of pain

Name: Anaximander
Notes: First philosopher known to write in prose; proposed the apeiron (the indefinite) as the first principle

Name: Anaximenes of Miletus
Notes: Held that air is the primary substance underlying all things

Name: Andronicus of Rhodes
Notes: Edited and organised Aristotle's works into the corpus still read today; coined the term 'metaphysics'

Name: Anne Conway
Notes: English Platonist and metaphysician; wrote Principles of the Most Ancient and Modern Philosophy; influenced Leibniz; converted to Quakerism; first woman in the Cambridge Platonist circle

Name: Anniceris
Notes: Modified Cyrenaic hedonism to allow for altruistic pleasure; redeemed Plato from slavery in Aegina

Name: Anselm
Notes: Archbishop of Canterbury; developed the ontological argument for God's existence; 'faith seeking understanding'; pivotal figure in Scholasticism

Name: Antiphon
Notes: Sophist and orator; wrote On Truth (fragment survives); argued for the natural equality of Greeks and barbarians; possibly the same as Antiphon the orator

Name: Antisthenes
Notes: Founder of Cynicism; held virtue alone is sufficient for happiness and that it cannot be taught, only demonstrated

Name: Arete of Cyrene
Notes: Daughter and student of Aristippus; mother and teacher of Aristippus the Younger; wrote 40 books according to Diogenes Laërtius

Name: Aristarchus of Samos
School 2:
presented the first known model that placed the Sun at the center of the 
known universe with the Earth revolving around it.
Notes:
presented the first known model that placed the Sun at the center of the 
known universe with the Earth revolving around it.

Name: Aristotle
Notes: founder of Peripatetic school; student of Plato

Name: Arrian of Nicomedia
Notes: Stoic philosopher and historian; compiled and edited the Discourses and Enchiridion of Epictetus; also wrote the Anabasis of Alexander

Name: Aspasia of Miletus
Notes: Intellectual companion of Pericles; ran a school of philosophy and rhetoric in Athens; cited by Plato as Socrates' teacher of rhetoric and erotics (Menexenus, Symposium); first woman associated with Socratic circles

Name: Augustine of Hippo
Teacher: Ambrose of Milan; influenced by Plotinus and Porphyry
Notes: Bishop of Hippo; synthesised Neoplatonism with Christian theology; author of Confessions and The City of God

Name: Averroes
Notes: Andalusian-Arab philosopher; full name Ibn Rushd; known in Latin West as 'The Commentator' on Aristotle; his commentaries profoundly shaped Latin Scholasticism; wrote The Incoherence of the Incoherence against Al-Ghazali

Name: Avicenna
Notes: Persian polymath; full name Ibn Sina; most influential Islamic philosopher; his Neoplatonist emanation cosmology shaped medieval Islamic and Jewish theology; developed the 'floating man' argument for the self-evident nature of the soul; wrote the Book of Healing and the Canon of Medicine

Name: Bardaisan
Notes: Syriac philosopher, poet, and theologian; founded a dualist school in Edessa; wrote The Book of the Laws of the Countries; synthesised Chaldean astrology, Stoic cosmology, and Christian theology; a key transmitter of Greek philosophy into Syriac culture

Name: Basilides (Stoic)
Notes: Denied the existence of incorporeal entities

Name: Basilides of Alexandria
Notes: Alexandrian Gnostic philosopher (distinct from Basilides the Stoic and Basilides the Epicurean); taught under Hadrian and Antoninus Pius; developed one of the first systematic Gnostic cosmologies with 365 heavens and the Abraxas deity; his son Isidore continued his school

Name: Basilides the Epicurean
School 2:
Succeeded Dionysius of Lamptrai as the head of the Epicurean school at 
Athens
Notes:
Succeeded Dionysius of Lamptrai as the head of the Epicurean school at 
Athens

Name: Benjamin Whichcote
Notes: English clergyman; considered the founding father of the Cambridge Platonist movement; provost of King's College Cambridge; his Aphorisms crystallised the group's core tenets: reason as the candle of the Lord, moral religion over dogma, latitude and toleration

Name: Berthold of Moosburg
Notes: Dominican Neoplatonist; wrote the only surviving full medieval commentary on Proclus's Elementatio Theologica

Name: Bertrand Russell
Notes: British mathematician, logician, and philosopher; co-wrote Principia Mathematica; early Platonist about universals; later moved to neutral monism

Name: Bessarion
Notes: Byzantine cardinal and Platonic philosopher; student of Plethon; defended Plato against Aristotle in the controversy with George of Trebizond; brought Greek manuscripts to Italy; his library became the foundation of the Biblioteca Marciana in Venice; key figure in transmission of Greek philosophy to the Renaissance

Name: Bion of Borysthenes
Teacher: Crates of Thebes; Theodorus the Atheist; Theophrastus
Notes: Once was a slave, later to be released

Name: Blaise Pascal
Notes: French mathematician and philosopher; Augustinian Christian with Platonic influences; wrote Pensées; developed early probability theory

Name: Boethius
Teacher: influenced by Ammonius Hermiae and Aristotelian commentators
Notes: Roman statesman and philosopher; author of The Consolation of Philosophy; executed under Theodoric

Name: Bonaventure
Notes: Franciscan theologian; synthesised Augustine and Aristotle with Neoplatonic elements; wrote Itinerarium Mentis in Deum; Doctor Seraphicus

Name: Calcidius
Notes: Translated Plato's Timaeus into Latin with commentary; only substantial Latin translation of Plato before the Renaissance

Name: Calippus of Syracuse
Notes: Athenian astronomer and mathematician; improved on Eudoxus' planetary model

Name: Carneades of Carnea
Teacher: Hegesinus of Pergamon; Diogenes of Babylon
Notes: Head of the New Academy; famous for arguing both sides of a case in Rome (155 BC)

Name: Cassius Longinus
Notes: Third-century rhetorician and philosopher; adviser to Queen Zenobia of Palmyra

Name: Chaeron of Pellene
Notes: Became tyrant of Pellene after studying with Plato

Name: Charmadas
Notes: Shared leadership of the Academy with Clitomachus and Aeschines around 110 BC

Name: Chrysanthius of Sardis
Notes: Refused Julian's invitation to Antioch; remained in Sardis

Name: Chrysippus
Notes: Third head of the Stoic school; prolific systematiser of Stoic logic and ethics; said 'If Chrysippus had not existed, there would be no Stoa'

Name: Cicero
Teacher: Philo of Larissa; Antiochus of Ascalon; Posidonius
Notes: Roman statesman and philosopher; Academic Skeptic; introduced Greek philosophy to Roman audiences; wrote De Natura Deorum, De Officiis, Tusculan Disputations

Name: Cleanthes
Notes: Second head of the Stoic school; author of the Hymn to Zeus; succeeded Zeno as scholarch

Name: Clement of Alexandria
Notes: Head of the Catechetical School of Alexandria; synthesised Platonism and Christianity

Name: Clitomachus
Notes: Carthaginian-born head of the New Academy; prolific author, none of whose works survive

Name: Crantor
Notes: First known commentator on Plato's Timaeus

Name: Crates of Athens
Notes: Scholarch of the Old Academy after Polemon

Name: Crispin Wright
Notes: British philosopher; neo-Fregean Platonist; defends abstractionism about numbers; wrote Frege's Conception of Numbers as Objects

Name: Cristoforo Landino
Notes: Florentine humanist and Platonist; wrote Disputationes Camaldulenses harmonising Plato with Virgil and Dante; professor at the Studio Fiorentino

Name: Critias
Notes: Athenian oligarch and writer; one of the Thirty Tyrants; student of Socrates and relative of Plato; wrote tragedies and elegies with philosophical content

Name: Crito of Athens
Notes: Wealthy friend and devoted disciple of Socrates; offered to finance his escape from prison; Plato's dialogue Crito is named after him

Name: Damascius
Teacher: Isidore of Alexandria; Marinus of Neapolis
Notes: Last head of the Platonic Academy before Justinian's closure in 529 AD

Name: Damo
School 2: reportedly the daughter of Pythagoras and Theano
Notes: reportedly the daughter of Pythagoras and Theano

Name: Dardanus of Athens
Notes: one of the several leaders of Stoa after the death of Panaetius

Name: David the Invincible
Notes: Armenian Neoplatonist; translated Greek philosophical texts into Armenian; wrote Prolegomena to Philosophy; known as 'the Invincible'

Name: Democritus
Notes: Co-founder of the atomic theory; held that everything is composed of indivisible atoms in the void

Name: Diagoras of Melos
Notes: Known as 'Diagoras the Atheist'; allegedly denied the existence of the gods

Name: Dionysius the Renegade
Notes: abandoned Stoicism for Cyrenaicism

Name: Dionysodorus
Notes: Itinerant Sophist; brother of Euthydemus; appeared in Plato's Euthydemus as a master of misleading argumentation and word-play

Name: Duns Scotus
Notes: Scottish Franciscan philosopher; known as Doctor Subtilis; developed formal distinction and the univocity of being (ens commune applies to both God and creatures); precursor of Ockham; innovator in epistemology and metaphysics; beatified 1993

Name: Edmund Husserl
Notes: German philosopher; founded phenomenology; his theory of ideal meanings and essences has strong Platonic affinities; wrote Logical Investigations and Ideas I

Name: Edward N. Zalta
Notes: American philosopher; developed object theory to systematise Platonic abstract objects; principal editor of the Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy

Name: Elias of Alexandria
Notes: Neoplatonist philosopher; probably the same as Elias bar Shinaya of the Armenian tradition; wrote commentaries on Porphyry's Isagoge and Aristotle's Categories; contemporary of Olympiodorus and David the Invincible

Name: Emanuel Swedenborg
Notes: Swedish scientist and mystic; claimed direct spiritual visions; wrote Heaven and Hell; his Neoplatonic metaphysics influenced Blake, Kant, and the New Church

Name: Epictetus
School 2: wrote *The Enchiridion*, a handbook of Stoic ethical advice
Notes: wrote *The Enchiridion*, a handbook of Stoic ethical advice

Name: Epicurus
School 2:
said that the purpose of philosophy was to attain tranquility characterized 
by *ataraxia*
Notes:
said that the purpose of philosophy was to attain tranquility characterized 
by *ataraxia*

Name: Eratosthenes of Cyrene
Teacher: Ariston of Chios; Lysanias of Cyrene; Callimachus
Notes: Polymath; head of the Library of Alexandria; calculated the circumference of the Earth; also studied ethics and cosmology; called 'Beta' (the all-rounder)

Name: Euthydemus of Chios
Notes: Itinerant Sophist; brother of Dionysodorus; appeared with his brother in Plato's dialogue Euthydemus; taught eristic argumentation for a fee

Name: Francesco Patrizi
Notes: Italian philosopher; the most systematic Renaissance Platonist; wrote Nova de Universis Philosophia (A New Universal Philosophy); proposed panpsychism and an infinite universe anticipating Bruno and Spinoza; clashed with Jesuit Aristotelians

Name: Gaius Marius Victorinus
Notes: African rhetorician and Neoplatonist philosopher; converted to Christianity c. 355 CE; translated Porphyry and Plotinus into Latin; influenced Augustine

Name: Giordano Bruno
Notes: Italian philosopher and Dominican friar; synthesised Neoplatonism, Hermeticism, and Copernican cosmology; burned at the stake by the Inquisition for heresy

Name: Giovanni Pico della Mirandola
Notes: Renaissance polymath; proposed a universal synthesis of Platonism, Aristotelianism, Kabbalah, and Hermeticism; wrote the Oration on the Dignity of Man

Name: Gottlob Frege
Notes: German logician and philosopher; founded modern mathematical logic and philosophy of language; his Platonism posited abstract objects as real and mind-independent

Name: Gregory of Nyssa
Notes: Bishop of Nyssa; one of the Cappadocian Fathers; deeply influenced by Origen and Neoplatonism; wrote Life of Moses and Homilies on the Song of Songs; developed apophatic theology and the concept of epektasis (perpetual spiritual ascent); canonised by both Catholic and Orthodox churches

Name: Harpocration of Argos
Notes: Student of Atticus; wrote a multi-volume commentary on Plato; known only through citations in later authors; contributed to the debate on the eternity of the world

Name: Henry More
Notes: English philosopher; Cambridge Platonist; corresponded with Descartes; developed concept of 'Spirit of Nature' as intermediary between God and matter

Name: Henry of Ghent
Notes: Franciscan-influenced secular theologian at Paris; known as 'Doctor Solemnis'; Augustinian Platonist who opposed strict Aristotelianism

Name: Heraclitus
Notes: claimed that "You cannot step in the same river twice" and "All is fire."

Name: Hildegard of Bingen
Notes: German Benedictine abbess, mystic, and polymath; wrote Scivias (Know the Ways) recording her visions; Neoplatonic viriditas (greening power) of divine presence in nature; also wrote on medicine, music, cosmology, and natural history; first female Doctor of the Church

Name: Iamblichus Chalcidensis
Teacher: Anatolius of Laodicea; Porphyry of Tyre

Name: Ibn Bajja
Notes: Andalusian philosopher; known in Latin West as Avempace; wrote Governance of the Solitary (Tadbir al-Mutawahhid); influenced Averroes and Maimonides; developed theory of the solitary philosopher achieving contemplative union with the Active Intellect without religious community

Name: Ibn Tufayl
Notes: Andalusian philosopher and physician; wrote Hayy ibn Yaqzan, the first philosophical novel; depicts a child raised in isolation achieving philosophical enlightenment through reason alone; synthesised Avicenna, Sufism, and Neoplatonism; his work influenced Spinoza and Locke

Name: Iris Murdoch
Notes: Irish-British philosopher and novelist; explicit Platonist; argued against linguistic philosophy and for a return to the Good as the central concept of moral philosophy; wrote The Sovereignty of Good and The Fire and the Sun (on Plato's theory of art); Platonic love as selfless attention to reality

Name: Isaac Israeli ben Solomon
Notes: Egyptian-born Jewish physician and philosopher; wrote Book of Definitions and Book on the Elements; first systematic Jewish Neoplatonist; transmitted Neoplatonic emanation theory into Jewish philosophy; influenced both Jewish and Islamic Scholasticism

Name: Jan Łukasiewicz
Notes: Polish logician; invented Polish notation and developed multi-valued logics; Platonist about logical laws

Name: John Philoponus
Notes: Christian Neoplatonist; wrote commentaries on Aristotle; challenged Aristotle's doctrine of the eternity of the world; precursor of the cosmological argument

Name: John Scotus Eriugena
Notes: Irish theologian and Neoplatonist; translated Pseudo-Dionysius into Latin; wrote Periphyseon (On the Division of Nature); most original Western philosopher of the early medieval period

Name: Josiah Royce
Notes: American idealist philosopher; taught at Harvard; wrote The World and the Individual; absolute idealism with Platonic influences

Name: Julian the Apostate
Notes: Roman emperor (361–363 CE); Neoplatonist philosopher; attempted to revive paganism; wrote Hymn to King Helios and Against the Galileans; patron of Sallustius

Name: Julius Evola
Notes: Italian philosopher and esotericist; synthesised Neoplatonism, Hermeticism, and Tantra in a Traditionalist framework; wrote Revolt Against the Modern World

Name: Justin Martyr
Notes: Christian apologist; studied Middle Platonism before conversion; employed Platonic philosophy in defence of Christianity; author of two Apologies and the Dialogue with Trypho

Name: Kurt Gödel
Notes: Austrian-American logician; proved the incompleteness theorems; explicit mathematical Platonist who believed mathematical objects are mind-independently real

Name: Leo Strauss
Notes: German-American political philosopher; advocated return to classical political philosophy; influential on neoconservative thought; wrote Natural Right and History

Name: Longinus
Notes: Philosopher and rhetorician; advisor to Queen Zenobia of Palmyra; teacher of Porphyry before Plotinus; possibly the author of On the Sublime

Name: Lucius Annaeus Cornutus
Notes: Roman Stoic philosopher; freedman of the Seneca family; teacher of the poets Persius and Lucan; wrote Theologiae Graecae Compendium, a Stoic allegorical interpretation of Greek mythology; exiled by Nero

Name: Lucretius
Notes: Roman Epicurean poet; author of De Rerum Natura, the most systematic exposition of Epicurean physics and ethics in Latin

Name: Macrina the Younger
Notes: Elder sister and teacher of Gregory of Nyssa; founded a monastic community on the family estate; Gregory cast her as the lead philosopher in his dialogue On the Soul and the Resurrection, modelled on Plato's Phaedo; the first woman in Christian tradition clearly depicted as a philosopher

Name: Maimonides
Notes: Full name Moses ben Maimon or Rambam; greatest medieval Jewish philosopher; synthesised Aristotelian rationalism with Jewish theology in The Guide for the Perplexed; also wrote the Mishneh Torah; his negative theology influenced both Jewish and Christian Scholasticism

Name: Mani
Notes: Founder of Manichaeism; synthesised Zoroastrian dualism, Buddhist ethics, and Christian Gnostic elements into a world religion; called himself the 'Seal of the Prophets'; his religion spread from Spain to China; executed by the Sasanian king Bahram I

Name: Marcus Aurelius
Teacher: Fronto; Junius Rusticus; Apollonius of Chalcedon
Notes: Roman emperor (161–180 CE); Stoic philosopher; author of the Meditations; last of the Five Good Emperors

Name: Marguerite Porete
Notes: French Beguine mystic; wrote The Mirror of Simple Souls arguing for the annihilation of the will in union with God; condemned as heretical and burned at the stake in Paris 1310; her work circulated anonymously and influenced Meister Eckhart and the Rhineland mystics

Name: Marsilio Ficino
Notes: Head of the Platonic Academy of Florence; translated the complete works of Plato and Plotinus into Latin; coined 'Platonic love'; wrote Theologia Platonica

Name: Mechthild of Magdeburg
Notes: German Beguine mystic; wrote The Flowing Light of the Godhead, one of the first mystical works in the German vernacular; bridal mysticism and direct union with the divine; influenced by Neoplatonism through Pseudo-Dionysius; influenced Dante

Name: Meister Eckhart
Notes: Dominican mystic and theologian; synthesised Neoplatonism with Christian theology; his mystical writings were condemned posthumously by Pope John XXII

Name: Michael Psellus
Notes: Byzantine scholar and statesman; revived the study of Plato and Neoplatonism in Constantinople after centuries of neglect; wrote De Omnifaria Doctrina and commentaries on Aristotle; drew on Proclus and Iamblichus for his theurgical interests; court intellectual under multiple emperors

Name: Musonius Rufus
Notes: Roman Stoic philosopher; teacher of Epictetus and Dio Chrysostom; exiled twice by Nero and Vespasian; advocated equal education for women

Name: Nicholas of Cusa
Notes: German cardinal, mathematician, and philosopher; wrote De Docta Ignorantia (On Learned Ignorance); developed the doctrine of coincidentia oppositorum (coincidence of opposites); precursor of Copernican cosmology and Reformation thought

Name: Onesicritus of Astypalaea
Notes: Cynic philosopher and admiral in Alexander the Great's fleet; recorded the gymnosophists (Indian naked philosophers) he met in the Punjab; wrote How Alexander Was Educated; conflated Cynic and Indian asceticism

Name: Panaetius
Teacher: Diogenes of Babylon; Crates of Mallus; Antipater of Tarsus

Name: Parmenides of Elea
Notes:
held that the only thing that exists is being itself; teacher of Zeno of 
Elea

Name: Paul of Venice
Notes: Augustinian philosopher; wrote Summa Philosophiae Naturalis; important transmitter of Averroist and Platonic ideas in northern Italy

Name: Penelope Maddy
Notes: American philosopher of mathematics; developed set-theoretic naturalism; wrote Realism in Mathematics and Second Philosophy

Name: Perictione I
Notes: Pythagorean philosopher; mother of Plato; attributed author of On the Harmony of Women and On Wisdom; argued for rational self-discipline as the foundation of womanly virtue

Name: Perictione II
Notes: Later Pythagorean philosopher (distinct from Plato's mother); wrote On Wisdom, a Neopythagorean text arguing that philosophy is the way to contemplate god and all things

Name: Peter Abelard
Notes: French philosopher and theologian; pioneer of scholastic method; developed conceptualism on universals; known also for his relationship with Héloïse

Name: Phaedo of Elis
Notes:
Originally founded the School of Elis; it was later transferred to Eretria 
by his pupil Menedemus.

Name: Plethon
Notes: Byzantine Platonist; sparked the Italian Renaissance revival of Plato; attended the Council of Florence; proposed replacing Christianity with a revived Platonic paganism

Name: Ptolemaïs of Cyrene
Notes: Female Pythagorean music theorist; wrote Pythagorean Principles of Music (cited by Porphyry in his Commentary on Ptolemy's Harmonics); synthesised Pythagorean and Aristoxenian theories of musical proportion

Name: Publius Nigidius Figulus
Notes: Roman senator and polymath; described by Cicero as the foremost scholar after Varro; revived Pythagoreanism in Rome; wrote on theology, augury, grammar, and natural science; companion of Caesar before falling from favour; died in exile

Name: Pyrrho
Teacher: Bryson of Achaea; Anaxarchus (companion of Alexander)
Notes: credited as being the first skeptic philosopher

Name: Ralph Cudworth
Notes: English philosopher; leader of the Cambridge Platonists; wrote The True Intellectual System of the Universe defending Platonic theism against materialism

Name: Roderick Chisholm
Notes: American philosopher; specialist in epistemology and metaphysics; defended Platonic realism about properties and propositions

Name: Sallustius
Teacher: Julian the Apostate (patron); influenced by Iamblichus

Name: Seneca the Younger
Notes: Roman statesman and playwright; wrote Letters to Lucilius and the Moral Essays; tutor to Nero; forced to commit suicide

Name: Severus
Notes: Middle Platonist commentator; wrote on Plato's Timaeus; cited by Proclus; held an intermediate position between Albinus and Numenius on the interpretation of the Demiurge

Name: Simon Magus
Notes: Samaritan proto-Gnostic; considered by early church fathers the father of all heresy; claimed divine status; his follower Menander developed his ideas; the simony of purchasing spiritual gifts is named after him; Acts 8:9–24

Name: Simone Weil
Notes: French philosopher and mystic; explicit Platonist who saw Plato as a proto-Christian; wrote Waiting for God and The Need for Roots; developed a spirituality of affliction (malheur) and decreation (kenotic union); also wrote brilliant political philosophy; died of self-imposed deprivation during WWII

Name: Socrates
Notes:
considered one of the founders of Western philosophy; credited as being the 
first moral philosopher

Name: Solomon ibn Gabirol
Notes: Andalusian Jewish philosopher and poet; known in the Latin West as Avicebron; wrote Fons Vitae (Source of Life) in Arabic, translated into Latin and widely read by Scholastics; Neoplatonist theory of matter and form; also wrote the ethical treatise On the Improvement of the Moral Qualities

Name: Stephanus of Alexandria
Notes: Last known Neoplatonist from Alexandria; invited to Constantinople by Emperor Heraclius; wrote commentaries on Aristotle and Plato; also wrote on alchemy and astronomy; his philosophical lectures mark the transition from Alexandrian to Byzantine Neoplatonism

Name: Stilpo
Teacher: Euclid of Megara; Thrasymachus of Corinth

Name: Suhrawardi
Notes: Persian philosopher; founded the Ishraqiyyah (Illuminationist) school of Islamic philosophy; synthesised Zoroastrian light-symbolism, Neoplatonism, and Islamic mysticism; wrote Hikmat al-Ishraq (Philosophy of Illumination); executed for alleged heresy at age 38; known as Shaykh al-Maqtul (the Murdered Master)

Name: Taurus of Beirut
Notes: Platonic philosopher who taught in Athens; teacher of Aulus Gellius (who recorded his lectures in Noctes Atticae); wrote commentaries on Plato's Timaeus and Gorgias; distinguished between different types of soul-entry into the body

Name: Thales
Notes:
first philosopher; held that the first principle (*arche*) is water; one of 
the Seven Sages of Greece

Name: Theaetetus of Athens
Notes: Mathematician and philosopher; Plato named a dialogue after him; contributed to the theory of irrational numbers and the theory of the five regular solids

Name: Theodoric of Freiberg
Notes: Dominican friar; gave the first correct mathematical explanation of the rainbow; Neoplatonist metaphysics influenced by Proclus via Meister Eckhart

Name: Theon of Alexandria
Notes: Mathematician and Neoplatonist at Alexandria; father and teacher of Hypatia; edited and commented on Euclid's Elements and Ptolemy's Almagest; his editions became the standard transmission texts for centuries

Name: Thomas Aquinas
Notes: Dominican friar and theologian; synthesised Aristotelian philosophy with Christian theology in the Summa Theologica; developed natural theology and the Five Ways for God's existence; Doctor Angelicus; most influential Scholastic philosopher

Name: Thomas Taylor
Notes: English translator and Neoplatonist; first to translate the complete works of Plato and Aristotle into English; translated Plotinus, Porphyry, Iamblichus, and Proclus

Name: Thrasyllus of Mendes
Notes: Egyptian-born Platonist and astrologer; arranged Plato's dialogues into the nine tetralogies still used today; court astrologer to the emperor Tiberius; also arranged the works of Democritus

Name: Valentinus
Notes: Egyptian Gnostic philosopher; most influential Gnostic thinker; founded the Valentinian school; came close to becoming Bishop of Rome c. 143 CE; developed elaborate Pleroma cosmology of divine emanations (Aeons); deeply influenced by Neoplatonism and Platonism

Name: W. V. O. Quine
Notes: American logician and philosopher; defended a naturalised Platonism about abstract objects through the indispensability argument

Name: William of Ockham
Notes: English Franciscan friar; father of nominalism; Ockham's Razor (entities should not be multiplied beyond necessity); challenged papal authority; rejected universals as mental concepts only; his voluntarist theology influenced Protestant Reformation

Name: Xenophanes of Colophon
Notes:
claimed that if oxen were able to imagine gods, those gods would be in the 
image of oxen

Name: Xenophilus
Notes: friend and teacher of Aristoxenus

Name: Zacharias Scholasticus
Notes: Christian Neoplatonist philosopher from Caesarea; studied under Ammonius in Alexandria; wrote the philosophical dialogue Ammonius (against eternal matter) and a Life of Severus of Antioch; his Ecclesiastical History preserves important information about late antique philosophy

Name: Zeno of Citium
Teacher: Crates of Thebes; Stilpo; Polemon; Diodorus Cronus
Notes: founder of the Stoic school of philosophy

Name: Zeno of Elea
Notes: famous creator of Zeno's paradoxes

Name: Zeno of Sidon
Notes: sometimes termed the "leading Epicurean"

Name: Zenobius
Notes: flourished in the times of the emperor Hadrian
